coredata.dc:title: Modified-graphene quantum dot thin-film nanocomposite membrane for enhanced nanofiltration performance and antifouling properties: Minimal incorporation
coredata.dc:creator: Chua, Siew Fen
Nouri, Alireza
Mahmoudi, Ebrahim
Ang, Wei Lun
Mohammad, Abdul Wahab
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD) with both oxygen-containing and amine functional groups was employed in this work to enhance the surface chemistry of thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane and cross-linking degree of polyamide (PA) layer for real wastewater treatment. The incorporation of NGQD has enhanced the hydrophilicity of all TFN membranes, as reflected by the decreased water contact angle (WCA) from 44.5 to 31.1°. Besides, the incorporation of NGQD interfered the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, and at the same time affected the surface tension between aqueous and organic phase, which caused the formation of thinner and rougher PA. The increase in hydrophilicity and surface roughness resulted in 17.5–65.5 % improvement of water permeability for TFN membrane which was attributed to the encouragement of water molecules attachment and transportation by incorporating hydrophilic NGQD. The membrane incorporated with minimal concentration (0.01 wt%) of NGQD (TFN2) has shown the optimum results. The TFN membranes fabricated displayed enhanced divalent salt (Na2SO4) rejection at above 95 %, especially TFN2 has shown 3.4 % higher rejection than unmodified thin film composite (TFC) membrane. For humic acid (HA) and congo red (CR) antifouling test, the flux recovery ratio (FRR) was improved by 3–14.4 %, while flux decline rate (FDR) was reduced by 5.4–22.1 % even after 3 cycles of filtration (FRR3). For real wastewater, aerobically-treated palm oil mill effluent (AT-POME) antifouling test, TFN2 has achieved the highest FRR3 at 75 % compared to TFC and commercial NF2001. With minimal incorporation of NGQD, antifouling properties were enhanced due to the increase of PA cross-linking degree and surface negativity by both its amine groups and oxygen-containing functional groups that repel and prevent the deposition of pollutants on the membrane surface.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (NGQD) with both oxygen-containing and amine functional groups was employed in this work to enhance the surface chemistry of thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane and cross-linking degree of polyamide (PA) layer for real wastewater treatment. The incorporation of NGQD has enhanced the hydrophilicity of all TFN membranes, as reflected by the decreased water contact angle (WCA) from 44.5 to 31.1°. Besides, the incorporation of NGQD interfered the interfacial polymerization (IP) reaction, and at the same time affected the surface tension between aqueous and organic phase, which caused the formation of thinner and rougher PA. The increase in hydrophilicity and surface roughness resulted in 17.5–65.5 % improvement of water permeability for TFN membrane which was attributed to the encouragement of water molecules attachment and transportation by incorporating hydrophilic NGQD. The membrane incorporated with minimal concentration (0.01 wt%) of NGQD (TFN2) has shown the optimum results. The TFN membranes fabricated displayed enhanced divalent salt (Na2SO4) rejection at above 95 %, especially TFN2 has shown 3.4 % higher rejection than unmodified thin film composite (TFC) membrane. For humic acid (HA) and congo red (CR) antifouling test, the flux recovery ratio (FRR) was improved by 3–14.4 %, while flux decline rate (FDR) was reduced by 5.4–22.1 % even after 3 cycles of filtration (FRR3). For real wastewater, aerobically-treated palm oil mill effluent (AT-POME) antifouling test, TFN2 has achieved the highest FRR3 at 75 % compared to TFC and commercial NF2001. With minimal incorporation of NGQD, antifouling properties were enhanced due to the increase of PA cross-linking degree and surface negativity by both its amine groups and oxygen-containing functional groups that repel and prevent the deposition of pollutants on the membrane surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution has become a pervasive global issue due to discharging different pollutants from different sectors into the water body [1]. To mitigate and overcome this issue, different treatment technologies such as adsorption, biological treatments, electrolysis, oxidation, membrane separation and filtration have been adopted [2,3]. With high efficiency, membrane separation and filtration technology have been one of the top pick options in water and wastewater treatment [4,5]. Among the different types of membranes, nanofiltration (NF) membranes have attracted the attention of researchers due to its filtration capability and efficiency, especially in salt rejection and pollutants (e.g., dyes and heavy metals ion) removal from wastewater [6]. These NF membranes could be fabricated either via the phase inversion technique or by forming an active layer on top of a porous support membrane through interfacial polymerization (IP). The latter approach is the dominant synthesis process for NF thin film composite membrane (TFC), and the crucial part of TFC is the active layer on top, which governs the membrane performance – water flux, impurities rejection, and fouling propensity [7,8].

To date, numerous efforts have been ongoing to tailor the membrane surface properties and the inner structure of selective layer, especially for minimizing the trade-off effect between membrane water permeability and salt rejection ability, while enhancing antifouling properties [9]. One commonly used method is membrane surface modification by employing nanoparticles as nanofiller during the IP reaction. The modified PA thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes have shown great improvement in water permeance with reduced trade-off effect while mitigating fouling [10–12]. These nanofillers usually facilitate rapid water permeation and at the same time block larger solutes from entering the nanochannels of membrane [13,14].

Carbon nanomaterials such as GO and carbon nanotube (CNT) are often functionalized with different materials to be incorporated in mixed matrix membrane or polyamide (PA) active layer of nanofiltration membrane. Among the carbon nanomaterials, graphene quantum dot (GQD) has begun to attract attention in membrane separation and filtration [15,16]. GQD is a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with abundant oxygenated functional groups that play an important role as highly active reaction sites [17,18]. In addition, GQD can be doped or functionalized with different materials, such as metal ions, nitrogen, phosphorus and other nanomaterials to further improve its properties (hydrophilicity or extra functional groups) or selectivity towards a specific species [19–21]. The additional functional groups, especially the amine group, could participate in IP reaction and increase the PA layer cross-linking degree [22,23]. This can be proven by Zheng et al. [24] in that the improved cross-linking of PA by incorporating 0.1–0.9 wt% of modified GQD has enhanced the divalent salt rejection by 2.2 %. At the same time, the antifouling properties (flux recovery ratio (FRR)) was increased by ~17 %.

In addition, Wu et al. [25] have synthesized L-aspartic acid functionalized GQD and incorporated them in PA layer. This work has improved the water permeability by ~60.9–100 % and demonstrated greater antifouling performance (~10 % increment in FRR) by incorporating 1000–4000 ppm of the nanomaterials. Nevertheless, trade-off effect became dominant in high loading concentration of nanomaterials. This has led to reduction in salt rejection which was eventually lower than the unmodified membrane due to a reduction in cross-linking degree of PA layer. In this case, when the loading concentration reached 4000 ppm, the divalent salt rejection, Na2SO4 reduced by 5 % as compared to unmodified membrane. Furthermore, it was reported that the utilization of high ratio of valuable nanomaterials is costly, while agglomeration might be an issue [26]. In the case of high nanomaterial loading concentration, it could cause membrane surface or pore blocking, which might lead to severe permeability reduction or increased permeability but sacrifice the membrane rejection ability [27]. In addition, most of the nanoparticles added would be discarded and wasted with the excess aqueous monomer solution during membrane fabrication [28]. Hence, the above observations indicate the need to incorporate minimal amount of nanomaterials in membrane synthesis to overcome the trade-off issue while avoiding agglomeration and the usage of excessive amount of nanoparticles.

Overall, besides minimizing the utilization of nanoparticles, it is also clear that even with low concentration, this might encourage amplification of membrane properties, followed by performance enhancement. Herein, NGQD was synthesized and incorporated in the aqueous phase of PA active layer via IP with different concentrations. In this work, the loading concentration was controlled below 0.1 wt%, which began from 0.005 to 0.075 wt%. The properties of all the membranes with or without incorporation of NGQD were analyzed with different tests. The NF performance of TFN membrane, pure water flux (PWF) and water permeability were evaluated and compared with TFC membrane. All the membranes were also tested with different divalent (Na2SO4, MgSO4) and monovalent salts (NaCl, MgCl2). The percentage of rejection were then compared between TFN and TFC membranes. To further demonstrate the capability of the TFN membranes fabricated with minimal NGQD incorporation, different synthetic wastewater, congo red (CR) and humic acid (HA) were used as foulants for the antifouling tests. Lastly, for the real wastewater antifouling test, aerobically-treated palm oil mill effluent (AT-POME) was used to test the limit and durability of the NGQD-incorporated TFN membrane fabricated in this work.

Urea and citric acid were purchased from Merck, Germany and SYSTERM, Malaysia, respectively. Polysulfone (PSF, Udel® P-3500) and both N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Solvay Specialty Polymers, USA and Sigma Aldrich, USA respectively. The non-woven membrane support (CU463) was provided by Neenah Filtration, USA. Commercial NF2001 membrane was purchased from Guo Chu Technology (Xiamen), China. N-hexane and both Piperazine (PIP) and Trimesoyl Chloride (TMC) were purchased from SYSTERM, Malaysia and Sigma Aldrich, USA, respectively. Different kinds of salts, Sodium chloride (NaCl) and magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) were purchased from SYSTERM, Malaysia, whereas sodium sulphate (Na2SO4) and magnesium chloride (MgCl2) were supplied by R&M, Malaysia and Chemiz, Malaysia, respectively. Humic acid (HA) and congo red (CR) were used as foulants and purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA.

The synthesis method of NGQD was adapted from previous published works [29,30]. A desired amount of urea and citric acid with the ratio of 1 mmol:3 mmol were well dispersed in deionized water. After ultrasonication for 30 min, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and allowed to react in oven at 160 °C for 5 h. After the sample was cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged under 8000 rpm for 15 min and dialyzed in a dialysis bag with molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da in deionized water for 48 h. The resultant solution was dried for 5 days and ready for use.

A dope solution with 82 wt% NMP, 15 wt% PSF and 3 wt% PVP was prepared. Firstly, PVP was added to NMP and stirred until fully dissolved in the solvent. PSF was slowly added into the mixture and stirred overnight at 50 °C to ensure homogenous mixing. After stirring, the dope solution was sonicated, degassed with a vacuum pump, and left for 12 h. PSF substrate was then cast on a non-woven support that was fixed on a glass plate using an automatic film applicator. The casted PSF substrate was immersed in a deionized water coagulation bath which allowed the solvent/non-solvent exchange to occur. The substrate was left overnight and washed with ultrapure water (UPW). The PSF substrate was stored in wet condition at the temperature of 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of TFN and TFC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: IP will be applied to form PA layer on PSF substrate, as shown in Fig. 1 . Monomers used were PIP and TMC. Firstly, PIP was added into deionized water, whereas TMC was added into n-Hexane and followed by ultrasonication for 45 min. The PSF substrate was clamped between a glass plate and a rubber frame. PIP aqueous solution was poured on the top surface of the substrate and allowed to contact for 5 min. A roller was used to roll off the residual droplets. TMC in n-hexane solution was poured onto the substrate surface and left for 1 min before draining it off. The membrane with PA layer was further treated in oven at 60 °C for 10 min. The TFC membrane was thoroughly rinsed with UPW and stored in UPW at 4 °C prior to use. TFN membrane are TFC membrane with different concentrations of NGQD synthesized as an additive in the PIP aqueous phase, shown in Table 1 .

The morphological characterization of NGQD was performed using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) with a model of TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN at 200 kV. In order to determine the crystallinity of NGQD, X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile was obtained by using a Bruker (D8 advance) X-ray diffractometer with a scanning range of 10° to 60° (2θ). The optical properties and the ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured by a UV/VIS/NIR spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Lambda 950). The surface elemental composition and distinctive bonding of NGQD were further examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Physical Electronics PHI 5400 spectrometer, Mimos Semiconductors).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TFC and NGQD-incorporated TFN membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Merlin Compact, ZEISS) was used to visualized the surface, and cross-sectional morphologies of TFC and TFN membrane fabricated. Atomic force microscope (AFM, NX-10, Park Systems) was employed to analyze the surface roughness of the membranes with a scanning area of 10 μm × 10 μm. The surface roughness was determined in terms of average roughness (Ra), root mean square roughness (Rrms) and surface area difference (SAD). The chemical surface functional groups of all membranes were examined by using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet 6700 Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., MA). Each sample was scanned at the wavelength range of 400 to 4000 cm−1. The zeta potential measurement which determined the surface charge of the nanoparticles, NGQD synthesized and membranes fabricated were carried out by Zeta Sizer, Nano-ZS (Malvern instruments Inc., UK). The NGQD was diluted in water and the measurement was done in room temperature by following the instrument manufacturer standard procedure. The surface hydrophilicity of all membranes fabricated were characterized via static water contact angle. These tests were carried out by using a Drop Shape Analysis System goniometer (model DSA100, KrussGmbH, Germany). This set up was equipped with a high-speed camera, F046B IRF (Stingray, Germany). A water droplet with 10 μL was dropped on the membrane surface and the image was captured and water contact angle was analyzed by the software provided. Using an x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Physical Electronics PHI 5400 spectrometer, Mimos Semiconductors), the surface elemental composition and unique bonding of the membranes were further investigated. The elements atomic concentration obtained could be used to calculate the O/N ratio and the cross-linking degree of the PA layer, with the equations as below [31]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane permeability and salt rejection evaluation
ce:section.ce:para:
The performance of the fabricated membranes was evaluated by using a stainless-steel dead-end filtration cell (Sterlitech Corp, HP4750). The membrane was firstly compacted by UPW at 6 bar until a steady flux was achieved. The pure water flux for each membrane was recorded at the operating pressure of 2, 3 and 4 bar for 20 min. The pure water flux (PWF, J) and permeability (WP) could be calculated as follow:

(5)

J ΔP
where V is volume of permeate collected over time, t and A is the total effective area of membrane (effective area = 14.6 cm2), while ΔP is the pressure applied.

The salt rejection (R) of all the membranes fabricated for different salts (NaCl, Na2SO4, MgCl2, MgSO4) with a concentration of 1000 ppm were tested individually at 4 bar. From the R obtained, the monovalent/divalent salt selectivity (SNaCl/Na2SO4, SMgCl2/MgSO4) could be calculated as below:

where Cf is the final salt concentration in permeate and Ci is the initial feed salt concentration. The salt conductivity was recorded by conductivity meter (Hanna Instruments, HI8633).

In the fouling test, HA and CR were used as foulants and tested separately. Before initiating the test, the membranes were pre-compacted for 30 min and the PWF (Jwi) was recorded. For each foulant, the fouling test was conducted for 3 cycles at 4 bar, which consists of 2 h of foulant filtration with the foulant flux (JF1,2,3) was recorded and then followed by 10 min of washing with RO water under rapid stirring in the dead-end cell. The PWF (Jw1,2,3) each time after the foulant filtration and washing was recorded. The rejection, (RF(HA), RF(CR)) of the foulants was also recorded by measuring the UV absorbance for their concentration via a spectrophotometer (Spectroquant® Prove 300). To determine and access the antifouling performance of the membranes, the following parameters were determined:

In this test, raw AT-POME was collected from Tennamaram Palm Oil Mill, Selangor, Malaysia, as the foulant. This test was conducted for 3 cycles at 4 bar, in which each cycle consisted of 4 h of foulant filtration, followed by 10 min of washing with RO water (without pressurising). The PWF after each cycle of fouling test and washing was recorded. The parameters calculated in this work were similar to the previous section, in which Eqs. (8)–(11) were applicable in this section. Also, as shown in Table 2 , the AT-POME was tested for its initial colour, total suspended solid (TSS), turbidity and chemical oxygen demand (COD, by using Hach DRB200, Dry Thermostat Reactor and Hach DR3900, spectrophotometer) to be compared with the permeate at the end of the fouling test.

HRTEM was carried out to observe the structure and determine the distribution size of the NGQDs synthesized. A typical HRTEM images of the acquired NGQDs is shown in Fig. 2a and b. The size distribution plot in Fig. 2c was done by measuring 100 dots. This reveals that the size distribution of the obtained NGQD ranged between 2.25 and 4.75 nm, with an average size of 3.35 nm. On the other hand, Fig. 2d showed the lattice spacing of synthesized NGQD. It exhibits excellent crystallinity with lattice fringe with spacing of 0.244 nm, which corresponds to (100) facet of graphitic carbon [32,33].

As shown in Fig. 3a, the XRD profile shows the broad peak at 26.8°, which corresponds to (002) diffraction peak with interlayer spacing of 0.357 nm. This also correlates to graphite lattice and disordered carbon atoms [34]. Fig. 3b shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NGQDs. From the results obtained, it consists of two main features: one shoulder and one peak. The absorption shoulder at 235 nm represents the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain or the CC bonds, whereas the peak at 334 nm could be related to n-π* transition of CO bonds [35]. In order to verify the surface composition of the synthesized NGQD, XPS spectra was determined as shown in Fig. 3c-f. The atomic concentration ratio for C:N:O is 59.59:10.05:30.35. It is noted that the existence of the O and N content is crucial as O content showed the synthesized NGQD is rich in oxygen functional groups, while the presence of N proves the N group from urea was successfully doped on GQD [36]. The N groups were also played an important role in IP reaction and enhancing the cross-linking degree which will be discussed further in the next section. These results and the peaks shown in different elements were in agreement with the results obtained by previous studies that synthesized NGQD [37,38]. Hence, from these observations, it can be concluded that the NGQD synthesized in this work is nitrogen-doped and rich with functional groups like amino, amide, hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TFC and TFN membrane

ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the surface and cross-section morphology of TFC and TFN are shown in Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b. As seen from the images, PA layer was successfully formed on the surface of PSF substrate layer, which possesses nodular morphology. The thickness of the PA active layer was measured and it can be seen that TFC membrane has the thickest PA layer at 184.3 nm compared to other TFN membranes. This depicts that the incorporation of NGQD in the PA layer resulted in the formation of thinner layer, in which the thickness was reduced by 21.2–33.3 %. With the aid of NGQD, it tends to draw more PIP to the substrate layer and causes PA nuclei to form at the beginning of the IP reaction, eventually growing into larger nodular structures [39]. Besides, this also favours the formation of a compact active layer [40,41]. The formation and thickness of the PA layer also depend on the diffusion rate of amine groups-containing monomer to react with acyl chloride monomer during the IP process. The incorporation of NGQD caused resistance to the diffusion of the aqueous monomer PIP towards the direction of reaction with the organic monomer, TMC [42,43]. Similar findings were also reported by Matshetshe et al. [23] and Seyedpour et al. [27] in which the incorporation of hydrophilic GO and GQD-based nanoparticles, it restricted the diffusion of aqueous monomer to react with TMC and eventually formed thinner layer. To further justify, with the incorporation of hydrophilic NGQD, due to the strong internal polarities of NGQD, PIP would be hindered from diffusing to the organic phase during IP. On the other hand, NGQD surrounded by cationic NH2 creates electrostatic repulsion with cationic PIP that could reduce the diffusion and eventually halt the IP [22]. Hence, a thinner PA layer was formed. Also, TFN with lower PA layer thickness is believed to contribute to higher permeability as the water molecules transportation path is shortened [44].

Fig. 4c shows the 3D AFM images of TFC and TFN membranes. All the membranes showed ridge-and-valley structures, with TFN membranes having rougher and higher Ra and RRMS compared to TFC membrane. Besides, SAD, which represents the rough feature frequency of membrane, also aligns with the trend of Ra and RRMS, in which these parameters increased after the incorporation of NGQD. The surface FESEM images in Fig. 4a also support these results. When the concentration of NGQD increased from 0.005 to 0.025 wt%, the average surface roughness and SAD increased as shown in Table 3 . However, as the concentration increased further to 0.05 and 0.075 wt%, the Ra, Rrms and SAD reduced. The decrease in surface roughness probably caused by the minor aggregation of the NGQD which limits the reaction of PIP with TMC [45]. Nevertheless, the aggregation is not severe as the surface roughness for both TFN4 and TFN5 was still higher than that of TFC membrane. The formation of PA layer by IP involves two stages in which, firstly, as mentioned, PIP reacts with TMC by diffusion across the organic solvent. Secondly, as PIP monomers continue to migrate and react with TMC monomers, the surface tension between the water and organic phases pushes and twists the developing PA layer, thus creating a ridge-and-valley structure [21,46]. Furthermore, the NGQD also has changed the surface tension between aqueous and organic monomer, which causes grainy and rougher surface formation. Besides interfering the PIP diffusion, NGQD also was forced out and extruded from the active layer to form agglomerates [22,47,48]. This then led to the formation of grainy surface and a significant rise in membrane surface roughness, which could promote and ease the water permeation and formation of the hydration layer.

Fig. 5 shows the surface chemical compositions of TFC and TFN2 membranes by FTIR. Only TFN2 is chosen to compare with TFC, as all the TFN membranes exhibit similar peaks regardless of concentration. For both TFC and TFN2, PSF support membrane usually exhibits peaks at 1248–1249 cm−1 and 1151 cm−1 which is attributed to asymmetric sulfone group, OSO and ether group, OCO stretching, respectively. The weak peaks at this region indicate that the PA thin film layer does not fully cover the PSF substrate. It is not a uniformly formed film instead, it is a cross-linked network structure formed on the substrate [49]. The peaks at 1622–1627, 1505–1506, and 1590 cm−1 which arise from CO stretching vibration of amide I group, NH bending vibration of amide II, and hydrogen bonded CO, respectively, prove the existence of PA active layer. The weak NH peak indicates thin polyamide layer is formed [50]. The broad peak at the region of 3404–3422 cm−1 corresponded to OH and NH stretching groups. By comparing the broad peak of TFC and TFN2, TFN2 peak is slightly intensified which could be caused by the incorporation of hydrophilic nanoparticles, NGQD. Nevertheless, no additional peak is shown in the TFN2 spectrum compared with TFC. This could be ascribed to overlapping the characteristic peaks of NGQD with the active or substrate layer within a similar wavelength region [13].

The TFC and TFN2 were analyzed using XPS to determine the membrane surface stoichiometry information and the related chemical environment. As seen from Fig. 6a, both TFC and TFN consist of three different peaks with different binding energy, which are 530, 399, and 284 eV for O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively, with their elemental compositions presented in Table 4 . For the narrow scan spectra, both TFC and TFN2 have similar peaks but different intensities. TFN2 exhibits higher N element than TFC which could be attributed to the incorporation of NGQD that is rich in amine groups. O/N ratio is usually used to determine the percentage of cross-linking of membrane. It is worth mentioning that the addition of NGQD, which contains elemental N and O might interfere and overlap with the peaks of N and O obtained from the XPS machine. However, due to the minimal incorporation of NGQD in this work, this overlapping effect is not as severe as other reported works which used higher loading concentration (as mentioned in Table 6). Hence, the cross-linking degree obtained using this data only could be used as supporting results for the following sections (membrane surface zeta potential and rejection tests). As shown in Table 4, the cross-linking degree increased after incorporating NGQD. C/N and O/C ratio were all calculated to further prove the cross-linking enhancement [14,40,51]. Besides lower O/N ratio obtained, TFN2 also showed lower C/N and O/C, which indicates higher cross-linking degree. The cross-linking degree improvement could be due to the terminal amine groups of NGQD which also participate during IP, and form extra chemical bonding within the PA matrix [52]. As mentioned, NGQD impedes the PIP diffusion which causes the formation of thinner layer, and this also might cause loose PA network forms. However, from the XPS results obtained, with extra amine groups available on NGQD, instead of loosening the PA cross-linked network, it enables more amine groups to take part in forming the PA layer. Besides, similar to previous study done by Kang et al. [40] which showed an reduction of O/N and improvement in cross-linking degree after the incorporation of nanoparticles. It was also found that the nanoparticles added was capable of attracting more PIP on the surface of substrate layer for the reaction with TMC. Hence, it explained that NGQD favours the formation of thin and dense PA layer.

Surface zeta potential is related to the surface functional groups or functional groups that are retained on top of the membrane surface. NGQD nanoparticles synthesized has zeta potential of −32.1 mV which reveals NGQD has negative surface charge. This is due to the presence of abundant of oxygen functional groups on the surface [53]. Generally, during the IP reaction, unreacted acyl chloride groups within the organic phase will be hydrolyzed into negatively charged carboxyl groups [54]. Hence, all the TFC and TFN membranes showed negative surface charged as presented in Fig. 7 . For the overall trend of membrane surface zeta potential, when the loading concentration is low, at 0.0005 wt% (TFN1), the membrane surface charge reduced to −19.1 mV due to the additional carboxyl groups available on NGQD. However, as the concentration of NGQD increased from 0.010 to 0.075 wt%, the membrane surface zeta potential gradually increased and grew higher than TFC. This could be caused by the amine groups and freely active hydroxyl groups of NGQD which participate in the IP reaction, consume the acyl chloride group of TMC and also form denser PA layer [55]. Thus, it increased the membrane surface charge slightly, yet all the TFN membranes were still showing negative surface charge.

From Fig. 7, incorporating NGQD in the PA aqueous layer has reduced the water contact angle (WCA) of the TFC membrane. All TFN membranes fabricated showed lower WCA than TFC membrane. Surface roughness is one of the vital factors contributing to the low WCA. According to Wenzel's law, for membrane with non-flat and hydrophilic surface (θ < 90°), as the surface roughness increases, the WCA decreases. The AFM results presented in previous section, the membrane surface became rougher by adding NGQD in the aqueous monomer phase. For all the TFN membranes fabricated with rougher surfaces, they have larger surface areas as compared to TFC membrane [40,42,56,57]. This enabled more water molecules attach onto the membranes surface and eventually led to low WCA. The WCA for TFN1, TFN2 and TFN3 declined as the concentration of NGQD added increased. TFC3 shows the lowest WCA, 31.1° which is 30 % lower than that of TFC membrane without the incorporating of NGQD. This could be attributed to the incorporation of hydrophilic NGQD. NGQD that is incorporated in the PA layer facilitates the transportation of water molecules due to its rich oxygen functional group [58]. However, when the concentration further increased to 0.050 wt% (TFN4) and 0.075 wt% (TFN5), the WCA increased. Nevertheless, the WCA for TFN4 and TFN5 are lower than TFC with WCA of 44.5°. The increased WCA for higher loading of nanoparticles is mainly attributed to the slight decline of surface roughness. The higher loading of nanoparticles could lead to the free volume of the top surface being fill by the nanoparticles and hence, increase the WCA [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeation properties and separation performance of TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PWF and WP were evaluated, and the results are shown in Fig. 8a. As the concentration of NGQD increased from 0.005 to 0.025 wt%, the PWF and WP increased by 30.3–63.9 % and 30.3–65.7 %, respectively. TFN3 has the highest PWF and WP of 63.9 LMH and 6.6 LMHB, respectively. Conversely, when the loading concentration increased to 0.075 wt%, the PWF and WP diminished. The increment decline was due to the agglomeration of NGQD. However, it was also noticeable that the agglomeration was minimal and the thin PA layer of TFN with high surface roughness was able to compensate for the agglomeration effect. Hence, both TFN4 and TFN5 achieved higher PWF and WP compared to TFC membrane. The overall PWF and WP enhancement of all TFN membranes were attributed to the enhanced surface morphology and hydrophilicity upon incorporating NGQD. The PWF and WP were aligned with the results obtained from FESEM, AFM and WCA. All the TFN membranes have higher surface roughness and WCA with thin PA layer. These led to TFN membranes having a larger area for water molecule attachment. At the same time, the NGQD could also create extra flow pathways for water molecules due to the existence of interface void between NGQD and the PA matrix [13,60,61].

From Fig. 8b, all the fabricated TFN membranes showed higher SNaCl/Na2SO4 and SMgCl2/MgSO4 than TFC membrane. The experimental results obtained obeyed the sequence of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl, which showed the typical salt rejection trend for negatively charged thin film nanocomposite membranes [62]. Salt rejection or the separation performance is often related to Donnan exclusion effect and molecular sieving mechanism. All the TFC and TFN membrane fabricated in this work showed higher rejection of Na2SO4 and MgSO4 than NaCl and MgCl2 which the Donnan exclusion effect could explain. According to the Donnan exclusion effect, negatively charged PA layer tends to repel high-valent anions like SO4 2− than monovalent ions, Cl− and attract high-valent cation, Mg2+ rather than Na+ [63,64]. While for molecular sieving mechanism, SO4 2− which has higher hydrated radius than Cl− would be easier to be retained on membrane [65]. However, in the case of MgCl2 and NaCl, size exclusion effect was dominant. The Mg2+ has larger hydration radius than Na+, thus the MgCl2 rejection was higher than NaCl which was similar to the previous finding obtained by Ang et al. [66] and Wu et al. [25]. Hence, these explained this work's overall trend of rejection of different salts.

In this work, all the TFN membranes achieved higher divalent salt, Na2SO4, 95 % and above, as compared to TFC membrane. As the concentration increased from 0.005 to 0.010 wt%, the divalent salts rejection for MgSO4 and Na2SO4 increased and reached the highest at 97.8 and 92.9 % respectively. As the concentration increased to 0.05 wt%, the salt rejection declined. The improvement of the salt rejection after the incorporation of NGQD was mainly due to the increased surface negativity and the improved cross-linking degree of PA layer as shown in the membrane XPS results. On the other hand, the reduction of salt rejection could be due to the less negative membrane surface charge and the interfacial void generated during IP reaction [45]. Commonly, TFN and TFC often face limitations like the trade-off effect between permeability and salt rejection. The increase in water flux or permeability is usually followed by reduction in selectivity or rejection performance. In Y. Liu et al. [13] work, the modified membrane has higher permeability than the unmodified membrane, while lower salt rejection was observed from the modified membrane. The nanoparticles added was found to serve as extra water pathways. However, at the same time, the interfacial voids created during IP reaction led to the reduction of the salt rejection. The scenario mentioned could be used to explain the decline of salt rejection shown as the loading concentration exceeded 0.01 wt%. As more NGQD was added, more interfacial void would be generated. As seen from TFN3, the improvement in Donnan exclusion effect only could hardly compensate for the weak molecular sieving mechanism. Hence, TFN3 has the lowest salt rejection for all salts among all the TFN membranes. Therefore, the salt rejection declined slightly as the loading concentration exceeded 0.001 wt%. Nonetheless, all the TFN membranes fabricated have higher permeability and divalent salt rejection compared to TFC membrane.

All the TFN membranes showed enhanced flux and permeability without sacrificing the salt rejection ability, especially for divalent salt and TFN2. This also could be attributed to incorporation of NGQD which enhanced the cross-linking and led to the formation of denser PA layer. Furthermore, NGQD is also tiny in size, less than 10 nm and has a much higher surface area-to-volume ratio than other larger nanoparticles and higher edge-to-volume ratio than other carbon-based nanomaterials like GO [67,68]. Thus, even if the incorporation concentration of NGQD is minimal, it could overcome the trade-off effect between membrane permeability and selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthetic water (HA and CR) antifouling test of TFC and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10a and b showed the normalized flux recorded throughout the antifouling test for 1000 ppm HA and 100 ppm CR for 3 cycles. In contrast, Fig. 10d and e showed their respective parameters measured, FRR, FDR, IFDR and RFDR. All TFC and TFN membranes have 99.9 % of HA rejection. On the other hand, for CR rejection, all the TFC and TFN membranes have 99.7–99.8 % rejection. All TFN membranes showed improved antifouling performance, with TFN2 membrane achieving the highest FRR (91.1 % and 92.9 %) and lowest FDR (18.1 % and 15.1 %) for both HA and CR antifouling tests. This could be attributed to the improved surface roughness, cross-linking degree and surface hydrophilicity. Similar findings were reported by Ng et al. [28], with the incorporation of nanoparticles in PA layer improving the FRR by enhancing the membrane surface hydrophilicity and roughness. Organic foulants like HA have a molecular weight of 2–500 kDA, while CR is an anionic dye. It is known that fouling of the membrane occurs when the pollutants trapped inside the membrane valley. Thus, the sieving effect and the Donnan exclusion effect of membrane worked together to produce its interception effect for its antifouling performance [40,64]. The large particles are unlikely to pass through the membrane. However, the small particles might enter and block the membrane pores and cause a significant flux reduction, eventually leading to severe fouling. The high surface negativity of all the fabricated membranes could repel the foulant actively. Furthermore, high surface hydrophilicity often results in more excellent antifouling performance since it can prevent hydrophobic foulants from adhering to membrane surfaces [57]. It could be seen from Fig. 10, the FRR increment and FDR reduction diminished as the loading concentration of NGQD exceeded 0.01 wt%. This could be because of the interfacial void formed during IP reaction, similar to the salt rejection tests mentioned. TFN3 showed the lowest FRR and the highest flux decline among all the TFN membranes. This could be attributed to a more interfacial void that allowed small particles to enter and caused a reduction in flux. Nonetheless, the high membrane surface hydrophilicity and surface roughness of TFN3 could compensate for this side effect. Therefore, TFN3 was able to achieve better antifouling performance than TFC membrane.

In a nutshell, the WCA and surface roughness parameters result obtained were consistent with the FRR and FDR results. With the incorporation of NGQD, all TFN membranes showed increased membrane hydrophilicity and surface roughness. The increased hydrophilicity and surface roughness tends to encourage hydrogen bonding between water molecules and membrane surface, and also attachment of more water molecules on the membrane surface. This thus promotes the creation of a hydrated layer that inhibits the direct adsorption of foulants on the membrane surface. When hydrodynamic shear force is introduced during the water cleaning process, this dramatically simplifies the foulant removal [28,69]. Hence, these explained the enhancement of FRR and reduction of FDR for all TFN membranes compared to TFC membrane.

The major components presence in AT-POME are lipids, fatty acids, lignin and the degraded products formed from crushed palm nut, tannin and humic acid [70]. Fig. 9a shows the raw AT-POME and treated POME by TFC, TFN2 and commercial NF2001. It can be seen that the rejection efficiency of both TFC and TFN2 was comparable to commercial NF2001, especially TFN2 has shown the highest removal efficiency with a reduction increment of TSS and turbidity as shown in Table 5 . This could be attributed to the incorporation of NGQD which enhanced the cross-linking degree of PA layer and membrane surface negativity, leading to higher rejection ability.

As shown in Fig. 9b, after 3 cycles of AT-POME filtration, TFN2 has the least fouled membrane surface compared to the other two membranes. This is also supported by the FESEM images taken as shown in Fig. c-e, especially the cross-section images, TFN2 too has been shown to have the least additional layer that was formed by the foulant. Besides, TFN2 also achieved the highest FRR, 75 % at the end of the test as compared to TFC and NF2001. It is also noticeable that TFN2 has the highest ratio of RFDR to IFDR among all the membranes tested. Hence, this explains the organic foulant of AT-POME is less likely to retain and deposit permanently on the membrane surface which might cause irreversible fouling and flux decline [71–73]. The added NGQD has played a vital role in enhancing antifouling properties as the more negative membrane surface charge and higher cross-linking degree have increased the membrane capability to repel the foulants and ease the membrane washing process. In addition, the improved hydrophilicity and surface roughness has also encouraged the formation of hydrated layer by water molecules on the membrane surface which helped to prevent the adsorption or strong attachment of foulant on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of previous studies with current work
ce:section.ce:para: In this part, previous studies on GQD- or CD-based incorporated NF membranes were compared with recent work as shown in Table 6 . For the comparison with current work, only membranes that involved IP reaction in fabrication were chosen. The trade-off effect between water permeability and salt rejection is expected as it can be seen in the previous studies mentioned in Table 6. Although water permeability of modified TFN membranes increased drastically, this often came with reduction in salt rejection especially in divalent salt rejection, Na2SO4. Compared to other works which used higher loading concentration, it is noticeable that TFN membranes fabricated in this work with 0.01 wt% of NGQD, showed high water permeability, Na2SO4 salt rejection and enhanced antifouling properties without causing any severe trade-off effect.

In this study, NGQD has been synthesized and incorporated into the aqueous phase of PA selective layer at different wt% (0.005, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.075) to develop TFN membranes with enhanced NF performance and for real wastewater treatment. This nanoparticle was hydrophilic and has a negative surface charge with a zeta potential of −32.1 mV. With the incorporation of NGQD, the surface roughness increased leading to WCA improvement, in which TFN3 has achieved the lowest WCA at 31.1°. All the TFN membranes also showed negative membrane surface charge which is favourable to rejection and antifouling tests. Consequently, pure water flux and permeability of all TFN membranes were improved by 8.1–13.6 % without significantly sacrificing in salt rejection. TFC and TFN membranes showed the highest salt rejection towards Na2SO4, with 3–14.4 % increment as compared to TFC. Both TFN1 and TFN2 have shown remarkable improvement in pure water flux and permeability (5.21 LMHB and 5.48 LMHB). For both HA and CR antifouling test, TFN2 showed the highest FRR3 at 91.1 % and 92.9 %, respectively, and the lowest FDR at 18.8 % and 15.1 % respectively. On the other hand, for AT-POME antifouling test, TFN2 has the highest FRR3 at 75 %, with the highest removal efficiency as compared to commercial NF2001 and self-fabricated TFC. Thus, it is clear that incorporation of NGQD, significantly 0.01 wt% into thin film PA selective layer could enhance the membrane properties and enhance the membrane permeance without compromising the salt rejection ability, especially the divalent salt. Hence, the incorporation of the minimal amount of hydrophilic NGQD has demonstrated promising development of hydrophilic membrane for enhanced nanofiltration performance and antifouling properties.
